text.title: Market Risk Categories and Scoring
text.summary: Five market-risk sub-categories with scoring rules and the citation-enforcement requirement.

rule.statement: Every risk row must cite a paragraph or table from an existing market research file (market-research.md, competitive-analysis.md, pricing-research.md, trend-analysis.md, niche-evaluation.md). No row may cite general knowledge.
rule.rationale: Uncited risk rows are opinion, not analysis. Citation enforcement makes the register auditable, prevents stale scores, and forces re-run when source documents change.

list.item: Demand: market too small, poor timing, no validated wedge (note: score against the validated wedge, not the TAM)
Competition: incumbent copies feature, well-funded rival enters, adjacent player pivots in (include not-yet-entered threats)
Pricing: split into four sub-rows — commoditization, price war, anchor mismatch, willingness-to-pay collapse
Trend: technology obsolescence, regulatory shift, macro headwind reversing the adoption curve
Channel: single-channel dependence (auto High-prob when one channel exceeds 40% of pipeline)

rule.statement: Score probability and impact independently on H/M/L (3/2/1), multiply for Risk Score (1-9). Force at least 2 rows at H/H (score 9) and at least 2 rows at L/L (score 1) — reject balanced-looking registers where every row is M/M.
rule.rationale: Symmetric scoring (all Med/Med) is the most common agent bias; forcing distribution prevents false balance and surfaces real exposure.

list.item: High probability: more than 50% chance in the planning horizon
High impact: existential threat to the segment or pricing model
Medium impact: major setback requiring significant resource reallocation
Low impact: minor inconvenience, resolved within one sprint
Avoid: eliminate the risk entirely (high prob + high impact); typically means not entering a segment or not adopting a channel
Mitigate: reduce probability or impact (medium risks); requires concrete actions with named owners
Transfer: shift to insurance, contracts, or SLAs (low probability, high impact)

rule.statement: Pair every High-priority row with exactly one of: kill-decision criterion, pivot criterion, or budget reallocation criterion. A risk row without a connected action is decoration.
rule.rationale: Risk registers that document threats without decision criteria are never acted on; the action criterion turns a log into a decision trigger.

rule.statement: Trigger metrics must be observable in the company's existing dashboard. "ARPU drops below $X for 2 consecutive months" works; "market sentiment shifts" does not.
rule.rationale: Unobservable triggers are never fired; the contingency plan is never activated; the risk register provides false security.
